name: Discovering the Buzet vineyards
name_original: A la découverte des vignobles de Buzet
description:
This trail explores the vineyards of Buzet, between the Garonne Valley and the hillsides bordering the Baïse River. The trail starts from the fortified village of Lavardac, once a crossroads for river trade. Now a marina, the village boasts an authentic rural atmosphere, perfect for a stroll.

The loop takes you through several major heritage sites, beginning with the largest fortified mill in France, the Moulin des Tours de Barbaste. Next come the Château de Xaintrailles and the fortified towns of Vianne and Damazan. The rural churches of Saint-Pierre-de-Buzet and Ambrus also punctuate the route. Between the two rivers, the landscapes vary from open vineyards to denser forests and flatlands.

The terrain is concentrated at the beginning and end of the trail. The central, flatter section runs along the banks of the Garonne in a calm and bucolic atmosphere. This circuit, when it does not use a cycle route, runs along small, quiet roads and offers a beautiful crossing of the region's wine-growing lands.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Lavardac traverse a landscape characterized by extensive vineyards and agricultural plains, interspersed with historical sites and river valleys. The region features a mix of gentle gradients and more challenging climbs, particularly around the Baïse river. This area offers varied terrain suitable for different road cycling preferences. Best road cycling routes around Lavardac The most popular road cycling route is Discovering the Buzet vineyards, a 47.9 miles (77.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 35 minutes to complete. This route explores the renowned Buzet wine region, offering views of vineyards and rural scenery. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Moulin des Tours de Barbaste – Saint John the Baptist Church loop from Lausseignan, a difficult 67.1 miles (107.9 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain and passes by historical landmarks and the Baïse river. Local road cyclists also love the Moulin des Tours de Barbaste – Bridge over the Baïse in Vianne loop from Lausseignan, a 52.3 miles (84.2 km) trail leading through historical sites and river landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 44 minutes. Road cycling around Lavardac is defined by vineyard-lined roads, historical mills and bridges, and the river valleys of the Baïse. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Lavardac are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 18 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Lavardac's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Lavardac?
A: There are over 140 road cycling routes around Lavardac, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. This includes 18 easy routes, 110 moderate routes, and 15 difficult routes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Lavardac?
A: Road cycling routes around Lavardac traverse a diverse landscape characterized by extensive vineyards and agricultural plains. You'll find a mix of gentle gradients and more challenging climbs, particularly around the Baïse river valley, offering varied terrain suitable for different road cycling preferences.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or a relaxed ride?
A: Yes, Lavardac offers 18 easy road cycling routes. A good option for a relaxed ride is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/35790260" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moulin des Tours de Barbaste – Barbaste Roman Bridge loop from Lavardac&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 36 km long and features minimal elevation gain, making it ideal for an enjoyable outing.

Q: Where can I find challenging road cycling routes with significant climbs?
A: For those seeking a challenge, Lavardac has 15 difficult road cycling routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/35696157" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moulin des Tours de Barbaste – Saint John the Baptist Church loop from Lausseignan&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 108 km route with over 1300 meters of elevation gain, passing by historical landmarks and along the Baïse river.

Q: What are some scenic road cycling routes that offer viewpoints or interesting sights?
A: Many routes in Lavardac offer scenic views of vineyards and river valleys. For historical sights, consider routes that pass by the Baïse river. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2092782583" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Discovering the Buzet vineyards&lt;/a&gt; route provides extensive views of the renowned Buzet wine region, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38547953" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moulin des Tours de Barbaste – Bridge over the Baïse in Vianne loop from Lausseignan&lt;/a&gt; takes you through historical sites and river landscapes.

Q: Are there any loop cycling trails around Lavardac?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Lavardac are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24833272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barbaste Roman Bridge – Moulin des Tours de Barbaste loop from Lausseignan&lt;/a&gt; is a 96 km loop that explores historical sites.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Lavardac?
A: The best time for road cycling in Lavardac is typically from spring to early autumn (April to October). During these months, the weather is generally pleasant, and the vineyards are vibrant, offering beautiful scenery. Summers can be warm, so early morning rides are recommended during peak season.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Lavardac?
A: The road cycling routes in Lavardac are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 18 reviews. Cyclists often praise the beautiful vineyard-lined roads, the historical mills and bridges, and the varied terrain along the river valleys of the Baïse.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or attractions to see along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region around Lavardac is rich in history. Many routes pass by notable landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5359707" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Henry IV's Mill&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3415519" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint Christopher's Church in Vianne&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5355928" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint John the Baptist Church&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1813247" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Buzet-sur-Baïse Lock&lt;/a&gt;, offering interesting stops during your ride.

Q: Are there options for road cycling routes around 50 km in length?
A: Yes, there are numerous routes around the 50 km mark. While the most popular routes might be longer, you can find many moderate options that fit this distance. For instance, the "Discovering the Buzet vineyards" route is approximately 77 km, but many shorter segments or alternative routes can be found or planned to meet a 50 km target.

Q: Can I find places for cafe stops or refreshments along the routes?
A: Given the region's agricultural and wine-producing nature, you'll often find villages and small towns along the routes where you can stop for refreshments. The Buzet wine region, in particular, offers opportunities to visit local producers or cafes. Planning your route through these towns will ensure convenient breaks.
tips: Magnificent 12th-century mill on the Baïse River, with its distinctive towers and Romanesque bridge. It is the largest fortified mill in France. It is open to the public from April to November.

The village of Damazan has retained all its charm with its central square and half-timbered houses. It's the perfect place to park your bike, enjoy a coffee in the shade, and soak up the atmosphere of the southwest.
The Buzet-sur-Baïse river stop is ideal for taking a break along the Canal Lateral à la Garonne. Perfect for cyclists, with plenty of supplies to stock up on!

Founded in 1284, Vianne is one of the rare bastides in the Southwest to have preserved its system of fortifications: its ramparts, gates and towers, practically intact today, have resisted the assault of time and men to constitute one of the most authentic testimonies of the medieval past of Albret. To see: the Vianne garden of Gontaut-Biron (in search of the mystery of the She-Wolf of Vianne), the atypical Romanesque church, the ramparts of the bastide along the Baïse river.

Montgaillard-en-Albret, perched on the hills of Lot-et-Garonne, is a small village that exudes authenticity. Its quiet streets and medieval heritage make it a pleasant stopover for cyclists seeking rural charm.
